1: Paul's arrival in Ephesus after the inland route finds 'some disciples' — the Christological community exists here before Paul arrives, but incompletely. The risen Christ has preceded his apostle; the twelve disciples of John are part of the anticipatory movement that the Christological fulfillment will complete. The Ephesian mission builds on what is already there, completing and transforming rather than creating from nothing.
2: Paul's diagnostic question — 'Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed?' — identifies the Christological incompleteness of the Baptist-disciples. The Spirit is the evidence of Christological completion: those who have truly encountered the risen Christ and entered the new covenant receive the Spirit. The absence of Spirit-reception signals that the Christological fullness (Christ's resurrection, exaltation, and Spirit-pouring) has not yet been received, though the preparatory repentance is genuine.
3: Their answer — 'We have not even heard that there is a Holy Spirit' — is a Christological gap: they know John's baptism but not its fulfillment. The Christological completion requires knowing not only John's preparatory message but the one John announced — the one who pours out the Spirit. Their ignorance of the Spirit is ignorance of the risen Christ's key post-resurrection act. The Spirit is inseparable from Christ: to not know the Spirit is to not know the full Christ.
4: Paul's explanation — 'John baptized with the baptism of repentance, telling the people to believe in the one who was to come after him, that is, Jesus' — gives the Christological orientation to John's entire ministry. John's baptism was calibrated toward Jesus; received in isolation from Jesus, it is genuine but incomplete. The Christological significance: all preparatory religious experience and sincere seeking must eventually come to focus on the specific person of Jesus Christ crucified and risen.
5: Rebaptism in the name of the Lord Jesus — the Christological name replacing John's preparatory baptism — is the definitive entrance into the new covenant community. The 'name of the Lord Jesus' is the invocation of Christ's personal lordship over the one being baptized. The old initiation was into repentance and expectation; the new initiation is into the fulfilled messianic community centered on the risen Lord's specific identity.
6: The Spirit's arrival with tongues and prophecy upon the laying on of Paul's hands is Ephesus's Pentecost — the last of Acts' boundary-crossing Spirit-outpourings. The Christological completeness: these twelve now share what the 120 received at Pentecost, what the Samaritans received in Acts 8, what Cornelius's household received in Acts 10. The risen Christ's Spirit is poured out upon every new community as it enters the Christological mission, regardless of its starting point or geographic location.
7: About twelve men — the symbolic number of Israel's tribes and the apostolic community — receives the Spirit. The Christological symbolism: a new mini-Pentecost with twelve is the reconstituted Israel pattern repeated in Ephesus, the far western edge of the Pauline mission. Every new Pentecost-echo in Acts reiterates that the Christological new creation community is reconstituted Israel expanding into all nations through the Spirit of the risen Christ.
8: Three months of bold synagogue reasoning about the kingdom of God — Paul's longest synagogue tenure — produces the usual division. The 'kingdom of God' (basileia tou theou) is the Christological content: the reign of God inaugurated in Christ's resurrection and extending through the Spirit's work. The Christological proclamation is always about the kingdom that has arrived in person; the rejection of Christ is rejection of God's own reign breaking into the world through the crucified-and-risen king.
9: The withdrawal to the hall of Tyrannus after synagogue rejection is the Christological community's characteristic adaptation: when the established institution closes, the mission finds new venues. The daily teaching in a hired lecture hall — available during the midday break — shows the risen Christ's mission using the ordinary structures of Hellenistic education to extend the Christological proclamation. Every venue is convertible into a Christological space; the Spirit is not limited to sacred buildings.
10: Two years of daily teaching in the Tyrannean hall means that 'all the residents of Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks' — the Christological ripple effect of one center reaching an entire province. The seven churches of Revelation (all in Asia Minor) were likely founded during this period. The risen Christ's strategy: plant one deeply rooted community (three years in Ephesus, Acts 20:31) and its influence extends throughout the surrounding region. Christological depth in one place produces Christological breadth across a region.
11: God performed extraordinary miracles by Paul's hands — Christological signs exceeding the ordinary. The miracles are 'through' Paul, not from Paul: the risen Christ's power operates through the apostolic instrument. The extraordinariness (ou tas tychousas dynameis — not the chance-level miracles) signals that the Christological endorsement of the Ephesian mission is superlative. The same Christ who worked miracles in his earthly ministry works them now through his commissioned witnesses.
12: The handkerchiefs and aprons touched by Paul carrying healing and exorcism power — Christological power mediated through physical contact with the apostle. The precedent is the woman with hemorrhage touching Jesus's garment (Luke 8:44), and Peter's shadow healing (Acts 5:15). The Christological principle: the risen Christ's healing presence is not disembodied but mediated through physical means, through persons who carry his Spirit. The incarnation established the pattern — divine power working through material means.
13: Itinerant Jewish exorcists attempting to use the name of Jesus as a magical formula — 'I adjure you by the Jesus whom Paul proclaims' — reveals the Christological misunderstanding that treats the name of Jesus as a technique rather than a relationship. The Christological name is not a spell; it is the personal authority of the risen Lord invoked by those who know him. The attempt to appropriate Christ's power without knowing Christ produces catastrophic results — the demon's response exposes the fraudulence.
14: The seven sons of Sceva represent the professional exorcistic tradition that tries to borrow Christological authority without Christological relationship. The Christological community's signs are not repeatable by those outside it; the power is personal, not technical. The fraud is exposed not by the apostles but by the demon itself — the spiritual realm recognizes the difference between those who know the risen Christ and those who merely know his name.
15: A direct revelation (through the demon's words): 'Jesus I know, and Paul I recognize, but who are you?' — the demon's acknowledgment of Jesus's authority is itself a Christological testimony, unwilling and ironic. The demonic realm knows the risen Christ's power and the apostolic community's authorization from him; what it does not recognize is unauthorized use of the Christological name. The spiritual hierarchy sees Christ's authority more clearly than human pretenders do.
16: The possessed man overpowering all seven sons of Sceva — leaping on them, mastering them, stripping and wounding them — is the Christological lesson delivered through defeat. Those who attempt to invoke Christ's authority without his power are not protected but exposed. The Christological principle: the risen Christ's name is not available for religious entrepreneurship; it is the personal authority of the living Lord, available only to those who are genuinely his. The humiliation of the Sceva sons is a mercy warning to all who would commercialize the Christological name.
17: The news spreading through Ephesus producing fear and magnification of the name of the Lord Jesus — the Sceva incident becomes the occasion for widespread Christological reverence. The Christological sovereignty: even the failed attempt to counterfeit Christ's power resulted in the genuine exaltation of Christ's name. The risen Lord can use the exposure of fraud as a means of authenticating his own authority. Every attack on the Christological mission that fails serves to demonstrate Christ's power more powerfully than success would have.
18: Believers coming forward to confess and disclose their magical practices — the Christological purification of a community that had been practising dual allegiance. The Christological lordship is totalizing: Christ's sovereignty over the believer's life cannot coexist with magical practice that invokes other spiritual powers. The confession and renunciation is the practical application of the baptismal transfer of loyalty from 'the power of Satan to God' (Acts 26:18) — the Christological life requires concrete, costly, visible repudiation of former spiritual allegiances.
19: The burning of magical books worth fifty thousand silver pieces — the Christological community's dramatic and costly renunciation of its former spiritual authorities. The books are burned rather than sold because selling them would perpetuate the spiritual harm they enabled. The cost is absorb consciously: the Christological claim on their lives is worth more than fifty thousand days' wages. The burned books are the Ephesian community's version of 'counting all things as loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord' (Philippians 3:8).
20: A theme: 'So the word of the Lord continued to increase and prevail mightily' — the sixth and most emphatic of Luke's growth summaries. The Christological triumph over Ephesus's magical tradition is the soil from which the mightiest growth formula in Acts springs. The risen Christ's word prevails (ischyen, was mighty, was powerful) — the word is not merely proclaimed but victorious. Christological strength is not measured by institutional size but by the spiritual conquest of rival systems that formerly held communities captive.
21: Paul's Spirit-resolved plan — 'I must also see Rome' (dei me kai Rōmēn idein) — is the Christological vision that will drive the rest of Acts. 'Must' (dei) is the language of divine necessity that runs through Luke-Acts: the Christ 'must' suffer (Luke 24:26), Paul 'must' testify in Rome (Acts 23:11). The Christological vocation is not optional; it is the risen Lord's direction that Paul follows. The same necessity that brought Christ to Jerusalem brings Paul to Rome — the pattern of apostolic witness matches the pattern of the Messiah's own mission.
22: The sending of Timothy and Erastus to Macedonia — the Christological mission's advance work before the apostle's arrival. The risen Christ extends his reach through multiple workers acting in coordination; the apostle is not the sole agent but the coordinator of a network of Christological servants. The Christological community organizes itself for mission: advance parties, follow-up visits, letters of introduction — all the ordinary instruments of the risen Lord's extraordinary mission.
23: The 'disturbance concerning the Way' (tarachos ouk oligos peri tēs hodou) — 'the Way' is Acts' characteristic name for the Christological community and its practice (Acts 9:2; Acts 19:9; Acts 22:4; Acts 24:14). The naming is Christological: Jesus declared 'I am the way' (John 14:6); his followers constitute 'the Way' by embodying his path of self-giving love in the world. The disturbance in Ephesus is not about doctrine alone but about a way of life that threatened the economic and religious order of the city.
24: Demetrius's complaint is nakedly economic: 'from this business we have our wealth' — the Christological mission is threatening his livelihood by making his product (silver shrines of Artemis) unnecessary. The risen Christ's displacement of idol worship is simultaneously a theological claim (there are no other gods) and a socioeconomic disruption (those whose income depends on the idol system suffer real material loss). The cross-shaped economy of Christ — self-giving rather than self-enriching — threatens every system built on extracting wealth from human spiritual need.
25: Demetrius frames the threat in terms of trade reputation before he mentions Artemis's honor — the economic motive precedes the religious one. The Christological insight: religious opposition to the gospel is often driven by economic and social interests that the gospel's advance threatens. The idol-makers' guild is defending its livelihood in the language of piety. The risen Christ's advance exposes the economic foundations of religious systems built on false worship; those whose income depends on idolatry predictably fight the Christological mission most fiercely.
26: Demetrius's description — 'this Paul has persuaded and turned away a great many people, saying that gods made with hands are not gods' — is accurate. The Christological proclamation necessarily includes the relativization of all other divine claims: 'there is no other name under heaven' (Acts 4:12); 'we ought to obey God rather than men' (Acts 5:29). The monotheistic Christological claim cannot coexist with the polytheistic market; Christ's lordship over 'all Asia and the world' (Acts 19:27) is the counter-geography that displaces Artemis's domain.
27: Demetrius's appeal to Artemis's honor alongside the economic threat — 'the temple of the great goddess Artemis may be counted as nothing' — reveals that the idol represents the entire civic, spiritual, and economic identity of the city. The Christological mission is not just changing individual beliefs; it is displacing an entire cosmic ordering. When Artemis's honor is 'counted as nothing,' the Ephesian world-order collapses. The risen Christ's advance in any culture produces exactly this: the existing spiritual order is relativized, and those whose identity was formed within that order resist.
28: The crowd's chant — 'Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!' — is the liturgical assertion of a rival cosmic order. The Christological community's proclamation — 'great is the Lord Jesus Christ' — is not stated in Acts but implied by the entire narrative. The chant and the gospel are mutually exclusive: only one cosmic claim can be ultimate. The riot is the clash of liturgies — two incompatible ascriptions of ultimate greatness, each with its community of worshippers. The risen Christ's claim to cosmic lordship always creates exactly this conflict with every culture's ultimate allegiances.
29: Gaius and Aristarchus seized as Paul's companions — the Christological community becomes corporately implicated in the apostle's proclamation. Those who associate with Christ's messenger share the risks of the Christological mission. The pattern recurs throughout Acts: the Christological community's solidarity means that attack on one is attack on all. Paul's impulse to enter the theater and face the crowd is the Christological courage that the disciples wisely restrain — the mission needs the apostle alive more than it needs a martyrdom demonstration.
30: The disciples and Asiarchs restraining Paul from entering the theater — the Christological wisdom of the community protecting its most productive witness. The risen Christ's sovereignty over the mission includes providential protection through human concern. The same impulse that drove Paul toward the theater (bold Christological witness) is tempered by the community's and officials' concern for his life — the Christological mission is not reckless martyrdom-seeking but purposeful witness that values the continuation of the proclamation.
31: Asiarchs — the highest-ranking provincial officials — being Paul's friends is itself a Christological sign: the gospel has penetrated the upper strata of Roman provincial society in Ephesus. The risen Christ's people include those at every social level; the 'not many' of high social standing (1 Corinthians 1:26) are not none. These friends' concern for Paul's safety shows that Christological relationships cross the deepest social divides of the Roman world — the apostle of the despised sect has genuine friends among the provincial elite.
32: The confused theater assembly — 'most of them did not know why they had come together' — is the Christological mission's implicit judgment on mob religion. Enthusiasm without understanding is not worship but chaos. The Christological community, by contrast, gathers with specific proclamation, clear understanding, and deliberate response. The riot's confusion stands against the Pentecost community's clarity (Acts 2:14 — 'Peter, standing with the Eleven, raised his voice and addressed them') as disorder stands against Spirit-given order.
33: Alexander the Jew put forward to dissociate the Jewish community from Paul — the Christological community's relationship to the synagogue community is exposed as tenuous in a crisis. When the mob's anti-idol fury might generalize to all monotheists, the Jewish community tries to distance itself from the Christian preacher. The Christological mission creates a new community that is neither straightforwardly Jewish nor Gentile — a 'third race' (in later Christian self-description) that requires its own standing.
34: Two hours of 'Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!' — the liturgical chant's duration reveals how much of the Christological threat to Ephesian identity was registered by the crowd. This is not rational response but identity-assertion: the chant is the city defending its cosmic narrative against the Christological alternative. The risen Christ's advance into any culture produces this: the culture's deepest commitments are revealed by what it reflexively defends under threat. Ephesus reveals that Artemis is its ultimate story.
35: The town clerk's appeal to Ephesus's unquestionable identity as temple-keeper of Artemis — the Christological displacement of the city's cosmic identity is something that even the civic official cannot acknowledge directly. His strategy is to assert Artemis's unassailable status as a way of defusing the riot — 'if Artemis's greatness cannot be threatened, then the Christians pose no danger'. The Christological mission is more threatening than the official can admit: the risen Christ actually is displacing Artemis, one conversion at a time.
36: The town clerk's call to order — 'you ought to be quiet and do nothing rash' — is the voice of Roman civic wisdom that, providentially, protects the Christological community. The risen Christ uses the instruments of Roman order (the fear of disorder, the threat of Roman intervention) to restrain persecution of his people. The Christological mission is protected not only by divine miracle but by the ordinary workings of civic prudence that God has established (Romans 13:1-7 — 'governing authorities are God's servants').
37: The town clerk's verdict — 'these men are neither temple robbers nor blasphemers of our goddess' — is the Christological community's legal exoneration by the civic authorities. The risen Christ's community had not violated Roman law or committed sacrilege against the civic cult. The legal innocence established here parallels every other Roman judicial finding in Acts: the Christological mission is not a criminal enterprise. Luke's apologetic is legal and theological simultaneously.
38: The town clerk redirects to proper legal channels — if Demetrius has complaints, the courts are open. The Christological principle: the risen Christ's community is not above the law but subject to it, and proper legal procedure consistently vindicates the gospel. The apostolic community in Acts consistently invokes legal processes (Paul's Roman citizenship appeals, Acts 16:37; Acts 22:25; Acts 25:11) rather than rejecting them — the Christological posture toward Roman law is engagement, not avoidance.
39: The regular assembly (ennomos ekklēsia — lawful assembly) as the proper venue for civic decisions — the Christological community's word (ekklēsia) is borrowed from exactly this civic assembly language. The Christian ekklēsia is the true 'assembly called by God,' the legitimate counterpart to the civic assembly — not parallel to Rome but under a higher sovereignty. The contrast between the riotous mob-assembly and the lawful deliberative assembly mirrors the contrast between the chaos of rival spiritual orders and the Christological community's ordered worship.
40: The town clerk's fear of Roman charges of sedition — 'we really are in danger of being charged with rioting' — is the providential mechanism that ends the riot and protects Paul's mission. The risen Christ's protection of his witnesses sometimes works through the political self-interest of city officials. The Christological sovereignty is not limited to miracles; it works through the entirely mundane calculations of officials who want to avoid trouble with Rome. Every instrument is available to the risen Lord for the protection of his mission.
41: The dismissal of the assembly — the riot ends where civic order reasserts itself — is the Christological mission's reprieve. Paul will depart Ephesus shortly, not because the mission failed but because it succeeded: three years of the deepest mission-work in Acts has produced a community (the Ephesian church addressed in Revelation 2:1-7 and Ephesians) that will outlast the apostle. The town clerk's gavel closing the riot closes also the Ephesian chapter; the risen Christ's work in Ephesus will continue without Paul's presence, because it was always Christ's work through Paul, never Paul's work for Christ.
